input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Infrastructure assets in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, such as buildings, represented as virtual models integrating static models with runtime data for simulation, analysis, and optimization throughout the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real-time insights, predictive analytics, optimization, and simulation capabilities for data-driven infrastructure management in the AECO domain, enabling stakeholders to make informed decisions across the building lifecycle from design to operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, utilizes Unreal Engine for interactive visualization, and employs Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a semantic backbone for structured data representation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensuring continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Modular architecture supporting multiple DT instances via standardized extension points; roles include Building Control Engineers (device modeling and deployment), Facility Managers (real-time monitoring and control), and DT Engineers (service integration), with the DT Framework as a centralized coordination hub
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Centralized DT Framework orchestrating components through modular architecture and standardized extension points, facilitating bidirectional data exchange and holistic building lifecycle management via runtime configuration management
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address interoperability across heterogeneous systems to ensure compatibility and data exchange between disparate technologies and platforms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are based on enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM) structures that incorporate semantic representations of Building Control System (BCS) components, including sensors, actuators, and controllers. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles transform these enriched BIM models into structured digital artifacts, enabling automatic generation of deployable DT components. Data sources include BIM models and real-time physical system data, managed through MDE-based transformation processes. The system supports geometric modeling for spatial layout, behavioral modeling for control logic, and analytical models via modular custom services for operational analytics.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange of live operational data between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The framework facilitates bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets by feeding control signals back to the Building Control System (BCS).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and utilizes relevant middleware metadata for Hono and Ditto.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling in BIM where Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers to establish a unified semantic foundation; Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to extract structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts; runtime operation with real-time data exchange for synchronization between physical and digital twins, interactive visualization for monitoring and control; and modular integration of custom services and analytics via standardized extension points for evolving operational needs. Lifecycle transitions are automated with minimal manual intervention, and governance is managed through role-based responsibilities of Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) is employed to transform enriched BIM models into structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture with standardized extension points enabling integration of custom services and analytics to adapt to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system supports user interaction through immersive visualization interfaces for monitoring and control of physical infrastructure. Analytics capabilities are enabled by integrated custom services via standardized extension points.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with AECO-specific standards including Building Information Modeling (BIM) for semantic modeling, and implementation of standardized extension points to ensure interoperability and service integration within the Digital Twin framework.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the definition of Digital Twins in the AECO domain provided in the text, specifically citing the integration of static models with runtime data for virtual representation and optimization. Supported by quotes: 'cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain'.

The description accurately captures the service capabilities and benefits detailed in the source, including real-time insights, predictive analytics, and decision support across the lifecycle. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle- encompassing optimization of building operations' and 'data-driven infrastructure management and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction correctly identifies and describes the roles of the key technologies mentioned in the abstract and introduction. It accurately attributes Eclipse Hono and Ditto to real-time data exchange, Unreal Engine to visualization, and BIM to serving as the semantic backbone.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic is directly supported by the source text, which explicitly describes the real-time synchronization capabilities. Quote: "Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts".

The extraction accurately identifies the roles defined in the source and the centralized nature of the framework. While the specific phrasing 'multiple DT instances' is an inference from the modular architecture, the roles and coordination hub are explicitly detailed. Quotes: "Table I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment Framework", "The DT Framework acts as a central hub", "The framework’s modular architecture, with standardized extension points".

The description is a highly accurate synthesis of the system's orchestration, components, and lifecycle management as described in the text. Quotes: "The DT Framework acts as a central hub", "facilitating... holistic approach to building life cycle management", "Runtime configuration management".

The extraction directly mirrors the source text regarding non-functional requirements for interoperability. It accurately describes the goal of integrating heterogeneous systems and ensuring data exchange. Quotes: 'our nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems' (Section III.A), 'framework... that satisfies semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and extensibility' (Design Science Problem section), 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' (Abstract).

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

block_6_judge.reasoning: The document discusses 'governance' in the context of model-driven engineering (e.g., 'MDE also strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies'), but does not provide specific details regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, data protection regulations, or consent management. Therefore, the extraction correctly identifies that this specific characteristic is not in the document.

While the text mentions technical components such as 'tenant and device ID or endpoint addresses' within the metadata, it does not describe specific cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms. The document focuses on deployment, interoperability, and visualization rather than security architecture. Thus, the extraction is correct in stating this is not in the document.
